title: Temporal Territories (protohistory, archaeology, linguistics — illustrative)
description: Time-bounded schematic extents: deep prehistory, protohistory, state formation, and teaching linguistics. Not cadastral boundaries; each feature has caveats. Append via tools/append-protohistory-territories.py

properties.name: Qin unification (approx.)
properties.description: Very coarse box over the Qin core and conquered Chinese states. Illustrative only.
properties.sources: Cambridge History of China; standard chronology (approximate extent).
properties.imageCaption: Flag map of the Qing dynasty (1820).svg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Old Kingdom Egypt (approx.)
properties.description: Nile valley corridor — highly simplified polygon.
properties.sources: Chronology from Oxford History of Ancient Egypt; geometry is schematic.
properties.symbolism: Colossal cut stone, royal tomb texts, and solar cult: pyramids as state theology cast in engineering.

properties.name: Maurya Empire (approx. peak)
properties.description: Simplified northern Indian subcontinent extent.
properties.sources: Romila Thapar, Asoka and the Decline of the Mauryas (extent generalized).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Achaemenid Empire (approx.)
properties.description: Large schematic rectangle from Anatolia through Iran — not a precise satrapy map.
properties.sources: Pierre Briant, From Cyrus to Alexander; schematic simplification.
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Hellenistic kingdoms (combined schematic)
properties.description: Eastern Mediterranean / West Asia simplified box; multiple polities merged for demo.
properties.sources: F.W. Walbank et al., Cambridge Ancient History VII (generalized).
properties.symbolism: Gigantomachy reliefs, ruler cult, and public libraries: kingship framed as saved cosmology in marble.

properties.name: Han dynasty China (approx.)
properties.description: Schematic eastern China extent excluding Tarim for clarity.
properties.sources: Hans Bielenstein, Cambridge History of China Vol. 1 (approximate outline).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Roman Empire (Trajanic peak, schematic)
properties.description: Two rough boxes for western and eastern Mediterranean spheres — not provincial boundaries.
properties.sources: Tacitus / CAH XI; commonly mapped maximum extent around 117 CE (simplified).
properties.symbolism: Spiral frieze, triumphal ludi, and civic basilicas: law and war narrated in stone around the capitol’s ritual calendar.

properties.name: Gupta Empire (approx.)
properties.description: North Indian heartland generalized.
properties.sources: Romila Thapar, Early India (extent approximate).
properties.symbolism: Buddha mandalas, Sanskrit court drama, and temple maths: a classical kosmos painted and patronised under rajas and sanghas.

properties.name: Tang dynasty (approx. core)
properties.description: Schematic eastern China; frontier belts omitted.
properties.sources: Cambridge History of China Vol. 3 (approximate).
properties.imageCaption: Caparisoned horse, Shaanxi or Henan province, China, Tang dynasty, 675-750 AD, earthenware with sancai glaze - Portland  (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Abbasid Caliphate (approx. high period)
properties.description: Schematic rectangle — actual control varied by region and decade.
properties.sources: Hugh Kennedy, Caliphate; simplified for demo.
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Mongol Empire (maximum schematic)
properties.description: Single vast box; real borders were fragmented khanates.
properties.sources: Morgan, The Mongols; extent greatly simplified.
properties.imageCaption: Genghis Khan’s empire at his death, schematic map (Commons).
properties.symbolism: Mongol postal relays, princely war-feasts, and trans-Eurasian prestige objects; the khanal court scripts multiple languages in parallel chancelleries.

properties.name: Mali Empire (approx.)
properties.description: West Sahel schematic — river-based polity not a crisp polygon.
properties.sources: Niane, Sundiata et al.; outline generalized.
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Aztec Empire / Triple Alliance (approx.)
properties.description: Central Mexican highlands — tiny box for demo scale.
properties.sources: Carrasco, The Aztecs; extent schematic.
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Inka Empire (approx.)
properties.description: Andean cordillera generalized box.
properties.sources: D'Altroy, The Incas; simplified geometry.
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Ottoman Empire (approx. Sulaymaniye-era)
properties.description: Anatolia to Egypt box — omits detailed Balkan and North African frontiers.
properties.sources: İnalcık, Ottoman Empire; schematic.
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: First French Empire (European core, schematic)
properties.description: Very coarse western/central Europe box; not 1812 detail.
properties.sources: Zamoyski, Napoleon; outline generalized.
properties.imageCaption: Napoleon I of France by Andrea Appiani.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: British Empire (approx. territorial zenith)
properties.description: Three coarse boxes (subcontinent, Australia, Canada) — excludes many smaller holdings.
properties.sources: Ferguson, Empire; colonial cartography summarized schematically.
properties.symbolism: Crimson atlases, exhibitionary machinery, and oceanic telegraph: empire rendered as a navigable, colour-coded system.

properties.name: Spanish American viceroyalties (schematic)
properties.description: Mexico–Peru rough boxes — not audiencia boundaries.
properties.sources: Kamen, Empire; generalized.
properties.imageCaption: Mapa, que comprende la Frontera, de los Dominios del Rey, en la America Septentrional.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Norse activity (very schematic)
properties.description: Northern seas and Atlantic rims — not a unified realm.
properties.sources: Haywood, Penguin Historical Atlas of the Vikings (concept only).
properties.imageCaption: Saga Oseberg Details ropes fore bow etc Viking ship replica 2012 Tønsberg harbour Norway 2019-08-16 04292.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Indus Valley / Harappan sphere (approx.)
properties.description: Mature Harappan cultural region — edges debated by scholars.
properties.sources: Posschl, The Indus Civilization (distribution generalized).
properties.symbolism: Unicorn seals, weights, and civic platforms; undeciphered script on seals—iconic administration and trade identity, not a temple empire reading.

properties.name: Corded Ware horizon (very schematic)
properties.description: North European plain — continuum, not a polity.
properties.sources: Furholt, 'Corded Ware' in European Journal of Archaeology (extent illustrative).
properties.imageCaption: 020210828 Vessels, Corded Ware Culture 5, Bell Beaker Culture 6, early neolithic artifacts in Silesia, Opole.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Bell Beaker phenomenon (schematic)
properties.description: Western/Central Europe spread — not a unified culture.
properties.sources: Needham et al., Bell Beaker transition (extent debated; simplified).
properties.imageCaption: 020210828 Vessels, Corded Ware Culture 5, Bell Beaker Culture 6, early neolithic artifacts in Silesia, Opole.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Olmec heartland (approx.)
properties.description: Southern Gulf Coast micro-region.
properties.sources: Diehl, The Olmecs; core zone simplified.
properties.imageCaption: Olmec Colossal Head, San Lorenzo, Veracruz, 1200-600 BC.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Yayoi cultural horizon (approx.)
properties.description: Western Honshu/Kyushu generalized.
properties.sources: Imamura, Prehistoric Japan (approximate spread).
properties.imageCaption: Bell (dotaku), Yayoi people, Japan, found in Yahagi, Wakayama, 20-250 AD, bronze - British Museum - DSC01445.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Chavín sphere (approx.)
properties.description: Northern Peruvian highlands/coast interaction area.
properties.sources: Burger, Chavin; schematic.
properties.imageCaption: A detail of the Raimondi Stela.png (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Mississippian cultures (schematic mid-continent)
properties.description: Cahokia-centered interaction sphere — not a single state.
properties.sources: Milner, Mounds of Cahokia; regional overview simplified.
properties.symbolism: Earthwork capitals, moon-calibrated maize, and copper-covered elites: a riverine world without Eurasian draft animals.

properties.name: Maya Lowlands (Classic period, schematic)
properties.description: Petén/Yucatán box — city-states not one realm.
properties.sources: Martin & Grube, Chronicle of the Maya Kings (area generalized).
properties.imageCaption: Tikal - Temple of the Great Jaguar.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Latin speech — Western Roman sphere (schematic)
properties.description: Romance ancestor zone before full dialect continuum — illustrative.
properties.sources: Herman, Vulgar Latin; areal linguistics generalized to a box.
properties.symbolism: Literary Latin on papyrus and wax, public capitals on stone: prestige epigraphy and school tradition as the hinge into Romance graphic culture.

properties.name: Romance vernacular area (high medieval, schematic)
properties.description: Iberia + south France rough — not isogloss-accurate.
properties.sources: Posner, Romance Languages (areal simplification).
properties.imageCaption: Grandes chroniques de France, Chanson de Roland (Commons).
properties.symbolism: Caroline and gothic book hands, chivalric codices, and vernacular song: reading communities and script shift away from classical orthography.

properties.name: Arabic — early Islamic expansion (schematic)
properties.description: Arabization is gradual and uneven — box shows a broad illustrative areal maximum.
properties.sources: Holes, Modern Arabic; Kaye, Hamito-Semitic (historical spread generalized).
properties.symbolism: Kufic and cursive traditions tie liturgy, law, and poetic performance; Arabic script becomes a prestige graphization across newly Islamized languages.

properties.name: Early Slavic dialect continuum (schematic)
properties.description: Eastern / Central European spread — not political borders.
properties.sources: Schenker, Slavic languages; historical linguistics map simplified.
properties.symbolism: Cyrillic (and related mission scripts) anchor church Slavonic; vernacular orthographies later multiply but share a common liturgical backbone.

properties.name: Germanic dialects — Iron Age / Roman era (hypothetical schematic)
properties.description: Reconstructed spread; exact limits unknown — marked uncertain in research.
properties.sources: Ringe, From Proto-Indo-European to Proto-Germanic (reconstruction; illustrative).
properties.symbolism: Runic epigraphy as short public and memorial writing; Latin script arrives for law and liturgy, but runes retain a counter-tradition on metal and stone.

properties.name: Bantu languages — early expansion phase (schematic)
properties.description: Sub-equatorial Africa — millennia-long process compressed here.
properties.sources: Nurse & Philippson, Bantu Languages (routes debated; box is illustrative).
properties.symbolism: Oral genres, drum and dance semiotics, and later Latin / Arabic / Ethiopic contacts: language spread is demic and contact-rich, not one script.

properties.name: Middle Persian / Iranian administrative languages (schematic)
properties.description: Sasanian and early Islamic Iranian spheres — merged for demo.
properties.sources: Lazard, Persian language; Daryaee, Sasanian Persia (areal generalized).
properties.symbolism: Pahlavi book and rock epigraphy under Sasanians; Zoroastrian liturgy and administration in a Perso-Aramaic graphic milieu before New Persian flourishes.

properties.name: Sahul — Pleistocene archaeology (illustrative)
properties.description: Continental shelf connection then rising seas; human chronology tens of millennia — box is a coarse teaching outline.
properties.sources: Hiscock, Archaeology of Ancient Australia; Clarkson et al. (2017) human occupation chronology debates.
properties.symbolism: Lakeshore cremations, megafauna overkill debates, and arid-land refugia: the continent’s first millennia as contested science.

properties.name: Late Glacial refugia — SW Europe (schematic)
properties.description: Simplified Franco–Cantabrian / Pyrenean refugium model — geography debated.
properties.sources: Straus, Magdalenian settlement patterns; climate archaeology syntheses.
properties.imageCaption: Western Europe in the LGM, schematic (Commons).
properties.symbolism: Mammoth-steppe, river corridors, and refugial art provinces: recolonisation routes structure later Gravettian and Magdalenian geographies.

properties.name: Late Upper Paleolithic — Atlantic Europe (bundle)
properties.description: Bundle of regionally distinct facies; merged for map demo only.
properties.sources: Gamble et al., The Upper Palaeolithic Revolution in Europe; site syntheses.
properties.symbolism: Mobiliary art, harpoons, and deep galleries: animals and abstractions as narrative or hunting-memory systems (readings differ).

properties.name: Epigravettian — northern Italy & Adriatic (schematic)
properties.description: Glacial—Late Glacial hunter-fisher-gatherer complexes on a coarse rectangle.
properties.sources: Mussi, Peopling of Europe; regional Epigravettian overviews.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Natufian — Levant (approx.)
properties.description: Sedentary foragers preceding Neolithic; dates vary by site.
properties.sources: Bar-Yosef, Natufian Culture; Belfer-Cohen & Goring-Morris summaries.
properties.symbolism: Morter basins, grave goods, and early sedentary signals before full domestication; modest bone and shell ornaments mark identity and place.

properties.name: Epipaleolithic—PPNB foreland — Zagros slopes (schematic)
properties.description: Long span over diverse facies; geometry is a loose western Iranian / Iraqi foothills box.
properties.sources: Hole, Zagros Paleolithic; later PPN sites (e.g. Jarmo context) for later end.
properties.symbolism: Caves and highland forager gardens lead toward Neolithic pack animals in the Fertile Crescent’s eastern arc—local sequences, not one wave.

properties.name: PPNA villages — southern Levant & Damascus basin (bundle)
properties.description: Jericho-scale settlements among others; boundaries schematic.
properties.sources: Kuijt & Goring-Morris; Finlayson & Makarewicz on PPNA transitions.
properties.imageCaption: Plastered face modeled on a human skull. From Ain Ghazal, Jordan. Pre-Pottery Neolithic B, c. 6500 BCE. Jordan Archaeolo (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Pre-Pottery Neolithic — upper Taurus / N. Mesopotamia (schematic)
properties.description: Göbekli Tepe–related horizon among others — not a unified polity.
properties.sources: Schmidt, Göbekli Tepe; Dietrich et al. dating debates.
properties.symbolism: Monumental T-pillars, fauna reliefs: pre-agricultural ritual display, possibly calendric or totemic; no public script in the later sense of literacy.

properties.name: Early Holocene — Fertile Crescent village farming (long schematic)
properties.description: Pre-state village networks; overlap with PPNA/PPNB layers on purpose for teaching.
properties.sources: Wenke, Patterns in Prehistory; Liverani, Ancient Near East overviews.
properties.imageCaption: Fertile crescent Neolithic B circa 7500 BC.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Jomon — Japanese archipelago (very long span, schematic)
properties.description: Hunter-fisher-gatherer pottery traditions; end date regional. Massive simplification.
properties.sources: Habu, Jomon archaeology; Imamura, Prehistoric Japan (chronology ranges).
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Paleoindian — Clovis & related (Central North America, schematic)
properties.description: Pleistocene terminus varies by calibration; not all cultures labeled Clovis.
properties.sources: Waters et al. dating debates; North American Paleoindian syntheses.
properties.symbolism: Fluted points, kill sites, and megafaunal extinctions: Paleoindian time depth without Old World neolithic sheen.

properties.name: Yangshao-related — middle Yellow River (schematic)
properties.description: Painted pottery village cultures before Erlitou—Shang consolidation.
properties.sources: Liu & Chen, Archaeology of China; Chang, Shang Civilization context.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Cucuteni–Trypillia — east Carpathian steppe fringe (schematic)
properties.description: Large tell societies; dates vary by phase regionally.
properties.sources: Monah & Popa; Chapman & others on Tripolye mega-sites.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Vinča / Tisza horizon — central Balkans & Pannonian fringe
properties.description: Neolithic–Chalcolithic tell cultures; merged for overview map.
properties.sources: Whittle, Europe in the Neolithic; regional site reports.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Mehrgarh — early Balochistan farming (precursor sphere)
properties.description: Long occupation sequence feeding Indus horizons; box is coarse.
properties.sources: Jarrige et al., Mehrgarh excavations; Possehl, Indus Civilization background.
properties.symbolism: Cereal harvesting, bucrania, and long-distance bead routes prefigure Indus chalcolithic—highland border of the 'Greater Indus' world.

properties.name: Uruk — early urban Mesopotamia (schematic maximum)
properties.description: City-state network; borders not those of a single empire.
properties.sources: Algaze, Uruk World System; Nissen, Early Near Eastern administration.
properties.imageCaption: Top register, Warka Vase, from Uruk, Iraq Museum.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Norte Chico — Late Archaic monuments (Peruvian coast)
properties.description: Caral among many sites; vertical societies along river valleys — rectangle over coastal strip.
properties.sources: Haas & Creamer; Shady Solís, Caral–Supe project.
properties.imageCaption: Mayor Pyramid and circular enclosure. Caral, Peru.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Lapita cultural complex — western Pacific (schematic arc)
properties.description: Austronesian seafaring marker assemblage; island geography highly fragmented.
properties.sources: Kirch, On the Road of the Winds; Spriggs, Islanders.
properties.imageCaption: Lapita potsherd from Fiji with face design.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Proto-Afroasiatic — deep hypothetical homeland range (illustrative)
properties.description: Highly contested glottochronology / phylogeographic proposals—shown only as a teaching band, not consensus geography.
properties.sources: Campbell & Poser (historical linguistics); Ehret, Afroasiatic; many competing models.
properties.symbolism: Deep-time reconstruction is disputed; iconic Egyptian and Semitic writing families show how Afroasiatic branches diverge in spoken form while sharing old areal ties.

properties.name: Tang dynasty China (approx. high point)
properties.description: Schematic eastern + western corridor; not equal to every Tang garrison map.
properties.sources: Twitchett, Cambridge History of China Vol. 3 (generalized extent).
properties.imageCaption: Portraits of Famous Men - Emperor Tang of the Shang Dynasty.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Gupta Empire (approx. northern Indian peak)
properties.description: Classical Sanskrit court culture; box is simplified.
properties.sources: Kulke & Rothermund, A History of India; CAH Vol. IV (generalized).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Umayyad Caliphate (approx. 750 CE maximum)
properties.description: From Iberia through Iran — merged schematic rectangle; not provincial detail.
properties.sources: Kennedy, The Prophet and the Age of the Caliphates; Hitti, History of the Arabs (generalized).
properties.imageCaption: Jerusalem-2013-Temple Mount-Dome of the Rock & Chain 02.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Mongol Empire (combined maximum, schematic)
properties.description: Very coarse Eurasian band; successor khanates excluded from single box.
properties.sources: Morgan, The Mongols; Cambridge History of Inner Asia (illustrative).
properties.symbolism: Khubilai and successor courts blend steppe, Chinese, and Iranian visual orders; the Ilkhanate sponsors Rashid al-Din’s world histories as imperial display.

properties.name: Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu, schematic Andean axis)
properties.description: North–south corridor along highlands; coasts simplified.
properties.sources: D'Altroy, The Incas; Rowe, standard syntheses (extent generalized).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Mississippian — Cahokia & American Bottom (schematic)
properties.description: Major mound center; rectangle over central Mississippi valley locale.
properties.sources: Pauketat, Cahokia; Fowler, The Cahokia Atlas (simplified).
properties.symbolism: Civic-ceremonial core of the Mississippian world: mounds, plazas, and ritually charged shell/copper that encode ranked alliance.

properties.name: Byzantine Empire (late 6th c., schematic)
properties.description: Eastern Mediterranean core under Maurice — merged rough outline.
properties.sources: Treadgold, A History of the Byzantine State and Society (generalized).
properties.imageCaption: Gaspare Fossati - Louis Haghe - Vue générale de la grande nef, en regardant l'occident (Hagia Sophia - Ayasofya Mosque n (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Early Sino-Tibetan — hypothetical Yellow River / Himalayan fringe (teaching band)
properties.description: Dates and homelands debated in phylogenetic literature; illustrative box only.
properties.sources: Sagart et al. (2019) PNAS; LaPolla & Thurgood, overview handbooks.
properties.symbolism: Chinese logography, Tibetic and Burmese Brahmic offshoots, and diverse smaller lects: hypothetical homelands pair with many independent writing histories.

properties.name: Indus Valley Civilization (Mature Harappan, schematic)
properties.description: Indus–Ghaggar–Hakra floodplains; twin urban cores generalized into one teaching box.
properties.sources: Possehl, The Indus Civilization; Kenoyer, Ancient Cities of the Indus (extent simplified).
properties.symbolism: Seals, Great Bath, grid streets: public measurability and water ritual as civic ideology (interpretations differ).

properties.name: Khmer Empire — Angkorian peak (schematic)
properties.description: Mainland Southeast Asia core; modern borders ignored.
properties.sources: Higham, The Civilization of Angkor; Chandler, A History of Cambodia (generalized).
properties.symbolism: Hindu-Buddhist baray and temple-mountain cosmograms; relief galleries display epic, royal dharma, and conquest as visible scripture.

properties.name: Carolingian Empire — Charlemagne (schematic)
properties.description: Western Europe core under unified Carolingian rule — rough rectangle.
properties.sources: McKitterick, Charlemagne; Nelson, King and Emperor (extent illustrative).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: West African empires — Songhai zenith (schematic)
properties.description: Sahelian bend and Niger trade axis; not a precise Songhai boundary map.
properties.sources: Hunwick, Timbuktu and the Songhay Empire; UNESCO Sahel histories (generalized).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Viking Age Scandinavia (homeland, schematic)
properties.description: Norse cultural core — Denmark, Norway, southern Sweden as one teaching box.
properties.sources: Sawyer, The Oxford Illustrated History of the Vikings; dendrochronology syntheses (generalized).
properties.symbolism: Oseberg textiles, stave churches, and rune-stone public memory: coastal prestige loops tie fjords to the Dnieper and Volga.

properties.name: Safavid Iran (early Shahs, schematic)
properties.description: Persian plateau and core provinces — illustrative, not Qizilbash administrative map.
properties.sources: Newman, Safavid Iran; Cambridge History of Iran Vol. 6 (extent generalized).
properties.imageCaption: Frame in the frame of Imam Mosque of Isfahan.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: South America — Iberian colonial sphere (schematic)
properties.description: Single teaching box over the continent during Iberian rule — not viceroyalty or treaty lines; use for era overview only.
properties.sources: Bakewell, A History of Latin America; standard colonial surveys (geometry highly generalized).
properties.imageCaption: 1795 D'Anville Wall Map of South America - Geographicus - SouthAmerica-laruiewhittle-1794.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Monuments, regalia, or map imagery paired with the extent for teaching; see description and sources for nuance.

properties.name: Colonial Brazil — captaincies & core settlement (schematic)
properties.description: Atlantic coast and interior penetration — illustrative; borders shifted constantly.
properties.sources: Schwartz, Sugar Plantations; Bethell, Colonial Brazil (extent simplified).
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata (approx.)
properties.description: Late colonial Southern Cone — Buenos Aires, Asunción, Potosí linkages; rectangle is not intendancy boundaries.
properties.sources: Lynch, Argentine Caudillo; Halperín-Donghi, Politics, Economics and Society (generalized).
properties.imageCaption: 1795 D'Anville Wall Map of South America - Geographicus - SouthAmerica-laruiewhittle-1794.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Tiwanaku & Wari — Middle Horizon (Andes, schematic)
properties.description: Altiplano / central Andean states and interaction spheres — merged teaching box, not two separate polity maps.
properties.sources: Janusek, Identity and Power in the Andean Tiwanaku; Isbell & McEwan, Tiwanaku and Wari (distribution generalized).
properties.imageCaption: Bolivia - Gate of the Sun detail - Tiwanaku or Tiahuanaco 02.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Amazonia — late pre-Columbian earthworks & mound societies (schematic)
properties.description: Geoglyphs, ring villages, and riverine polities across a vast area — one coarse basin box for overview.
properties.sources: Roosevelt, Amazonian archaeology; de Souza et al. (2018) earthwork surveys (extent illustrative).
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Quechua languages — Andean continuum (schematic, modern inclusive)
properties.description: Highland spine from Colombia to Bolivia — uneven shift to Spanish; shown as a teaching band, not census data.
properties.sources: Adelaar with Muysken, Languages of the Andes; Ethnologue summaries (areal simplification).
properties.symbolism: Knotted accounting and colonial Quechua alphabetic record: indigenous and European notations entangle in law, tribute, and sermon.

properties.name: Tupi–Guaraní languages — Atlantic & Paraná (schematic)
properties.description: Coastal and interior branches before and after Portuguese/Spanish contact; Paraguayan Guaraní co-official today — box is not dialect map.
properties.sources: Rodrigues, Tupi languages; Jensen, Tupí–Guaraní (areal generalized).
properties.symbolism: Jesuit and national orthographies, pictorial catechisms, and today’s vibrant Guaraní print: language survival is tied to visible public text.

properties.name: Gravettian — Central European mammoth steppe (schematic)
properties.description: Iconic art and structured hunting camps; one broad polygon, not sub-regional facies (Pavlov, Willendorf, etc. simplified).
properties.sources: G.E. Sieveking, Magdalenian; Hoffecker, The Early Upper Paleolithic of Central Europe; extent is pedagogical.
properties.symbolism: Portable female figurines in mammoth ivory—interpretations (fertility, seasonality, body ideals) are debated; part of a pan-European visual idiom.

properties.name: Solutrean — Pyrenean & French facies (schematic)
properties.description: Lithic bifaces and high-level hunting; coarse western Mediterranean box.
properties.sources: Straus, The Role of the Solutrean in Iberia; Solutrean-Hispanic research syntheses; boundaries schematic.
properties.symbolism: Cave polychrome animals, hand stencils, and abstract signs: parietal art as place-bound performance and possibly seasonal knowledge.

properties.name: Eastern Europe — Upper Paleolithic (Kostenki, Sunghir cluster, schematic)
properties.description: Eastern Gravettian and related; mammoth-bone housing and early elaborate burials; huge time span, one box.
properties.sources: Praslov & Rogachev, Kostenki; Pitulko, Paleolithic of the Kolyma; spatial extent is generalized.
properties.imageCaption: Twin burial model - Sungir - Vladimir Palaty.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Iberomaurusian & Capsian fringes (NW Africa, schematic)
properties.description: Post-LGM reoccupation; backed bladelets, shell middens, Epipalaeolithic–early Holocene in Maghrib.
properties.sources: Barton & colleagues, LGM refugia and re-expansion; Garcea, Saharan Crossroads; outline coarse.
properties.symbolism: Backed blades, ostrich-eggshell beads, and open-air art in arid refugia: Maghrebian technocomplexes tie mobility to water and view-sheds.

properties.name: Aterian MSA—Maghreb and Nile approaches (teaching map)
properties.description: Pedunculate points and tanged tools; broad span and area—compare specialist regional maps.
properties.sources: Garcea, Aterian: Techno-economic and environmental perspectives; timespan varies by site.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Southern Africa — Howieson’s Poort & later LSA (schematic)
properties.description: Bladelets, osseous work, and Holocene forager intensification; not a single culture—overview box only.
properties.sources: Mitchell, The Archaeology of Southern Africa; Wadley, Howieson’s Poort; region simplified.
properties.symbolism: Howiesons Poort, Still Bay, and later microliths; engraved ochre, shell jewellery, and coastal adaptation as negotiable ‘modern’ signals.

properties.name: North Sea — Doggerland & Mesolithic coastal plains (paleo-shore, schematic)
properties.description: Submerged plain now under the North Sea; Mesolithic scatters when dry. Shape is a modern projection box, not LGM bathymetry.
properties.sources: Amkreutz et al., Mesolithic in Northwestern Europe; Gaffney et al., Doggerland; geometry illustrative only.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: East Africa — rift and lakeshore foragers to pastoral transition (broad box)
properties.description: Hunter-gatherer, Later Stone Age, and early pastoral/iron transitions—several millennia merged for map literacy.
properties.sources: Lane, African Archaeology; Marshall & Hildebrand, Before food production in North Kenya; not tribal-scale.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: PPNB / Pottery Neolithic — Levant to W. Anatolia (villages & cattle, schematic)
properties.description: Megasites, plastered skulls, early domesticates; Aşıklı, Çayönü, ‘Ain Ghazal, Jericho (period overlap varies).
properties.sources: Garfinkel, Burian et al., Neolithization; Ozdoğan, Westward spread of the Neolithic; polygon generalized.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Central Anatolia — Çatalhöyük & Neolithic–Chalcolithic Konya plain (teaching area)
properties.description: Dense agglomerate settlement; art-rich houses; not to scale of individual mounds.
properties.sources: Hodder, Çatalhöyük; Mellaart legacy critiqued; areal map simplified.
properties.symbolism: Bull-horn benches, vulture reliefs, interior burials, wall paintings: life-death and wild-domestic cycled in domestic ‘shrines’.

properties.name: Halaf & Samarra-related — Northern Mesopotamia (schematic)
properties.description: Painted ware villages; Tell Halaf, Arpachiyah—interaction sphere, not a single state.
properties.sources: Akkermans & Schwartz, The Archaeology of Syria; Munchaev & Merpert on Halaf; outline coarse.
properties.imageCaption: Painted pottery jar. From Yarim Tepe, Iraq. Halaf culture, 5th millennium BCE. Iraq Museum, Baghdad.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Uruk period & early dynastic Sumer (riverine core, schematic)
properties.description: Cities, writing-precursor tokens, and temple economy; Tigris–Euphrates alluvial box.
properties.sources: Nissen, The Early History of the Ancient Near East; Algaze, The Uruk World System; oversimplified map.
properties.symbolism: Sealed clay envelopes, bevel-rim bowls, and temple-led redistribution: early cuneiform is accounting theatre before belles lettres.

properties.name: BMAC (Oxus Civilization) — Central Asian oasis (schematic)
properties.description: Margiana and Bactria: fortified sites, steppe and Iranian Plateau contact; not modern borders.
properties.sources: Hiebert & Kurbansakhatov, Central Asian village cultures; Sarianidi, Bactria-Margiana; area generalized.
properties.symbolism: Oasis fortifications, fire temples, compartmented seals: long-distance style mixing and elite ritual, not a single public epic tradition.

properties.name: Yamnaya — Pontic–Caspian kurgans & wagons (teaching area)
properties.description: Mobile pastoralist burial mounds; steppe block over Ukraine–Volga; genetics literature widely cited—interpret cautiously.
properties.sources: Frachetti, Pastoralist Landscapes; Anthony, The Horse, the Wheel, and Language; map not isotopic niche precision.
properties.symbolism: Cattle, wagons, and kurgan stelae broadcast mobile pastoral charisma; male-focused grave kits seed later steppe ideologies in archaeological debate.

properties.name: Sintashta–Arkaim — Ural chariots & early metallurgy (schematic hub)
properties.description: Walled settlements, spoked-wheel evidence; not coextensive with all Sintashta sites.
properties.sources: Anthony & Vinogradov, Birth of the chariot; Zdanovich, Arkaim; extent simplified.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Andronovo horizon — Eurasian steppe & oases (schematic range)
properties.description: Metallurgical networks across steppe; often linked in debate to later Iranian/Saka worlds—highly general box.
properties.sources: Kuzmina, The Origin of the Indo-Iranians; Hiebert, Central Asia; areal not genetic map.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Hittite Old / Middle Kingdom — Anatolia & Syria campaigns (simplified block)
properties.description: Hattusa, Syro-Anatolian polities, Mitanni interactions; not detailed battle-by-battle map.
properties.sources: Bryce, The Kingdom of the Hittites; Hoffner, Hittite studies; box schematic.
properties.symbolism: Lion iconography, Yazılıkaya processions, storm-god festivals: royal religion as cosmic maintenance through liturgy and sacrifice.

properties.name: Minoan palatial—Crete with Aegean thalassocracy hints (simplified box)
properties.description: Palaces, Linear A, Thera eruption horizon; thalassocracy debated—Cretan core highlighted.
properties.sources: Shelmerdine, The Cambridge Companion to the Aegean Bronze Age; Warren, Minoan Crete; not site-scale.
properties.symbolism: Bull sports, lustral basins, marine frescoes: palatial feasting, initiation, and the sea as symbolic horizon (no Homeric state here).

properties.name: Mycenaean palatial—Greek mainland & Argolid core (simplified)
properties.description: Linear B admin; citadels, shaft graves; not every LH III province.
properties.sources: Shelmerdine, Palaces and politics; Whitley, Greek archaeology; one merged polygon.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Shang — Central Plains bronze cities (Erlitou to Anyang, schematic area)
properties.description: Anyang, ritual bronze, chariot tombs, oracle bones; political extent debated—core floodplain only.
properties.sources: Keightley, The Ancestral Landscape; Thorp, China in the Early Bronze Age; extent pedagogical.
properties.symbolism: Bronze zun/ding in tombs, oracle bones on scapulae: king as chief diviner; writing as kingly and ritual archive.

properties.name: Western Zhou—Wei River & Guanzhong (early royal domain, approximate)
properties.description: Fenghao capitals, bronze inscriptions, classical Mandate of Heaven; not the full Zhou ecumene.
properties.sources: Shaughnessy, Sources of Western Zhou; Rawski & Rawson, ed., China, map generalized.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Eastern Zhou — Warring States (schematic, multiple polity extent merged)
properties.description: Hundred schools, cavalry, walls; Qin eventually unifying—illustrative blob over north China basins.
properties.sources: Lewis, Warring States; Loewe & Shaughnessy, The Cambridge History of Ancient China; not to district map.
properties.symbolism: Hundred schools debate, chariot inscriptions, and jade/ bronze burial theatre: state competition makes philosophy and arms races publicly visible.

properties.name: Dilmun / Tylos — Gulf entrepôts & trade (broad gulf map)
properties.description: Pearl fishing, seafaring, Mesopotamian & Indus linkages; Bahrain core + gulf schematized.
properties.sources: Crawford, Dilmun and its Gulf neighbours; Potts, Arabian Gulf; outline simplified.
properties.symbolism: Gulf entrepôts, cuneiform and Indus contact: trade prestige and stelae display; religious forms borrow from Mesopotamia and the Gulf.

properties.name: South Arabian kingdoms (Saba, Ḥaḍramawt) — fringes & Ethiopian links (broad map)
properties.description: Incense caravans, dam engineering, epigraphy; Aksum later interacts—simplified Yemen highlands and coast.
properties.sources: Nebes & Stein, Ancient South Arabia; Munro, Meroë and the Horn; political edges fuzzy.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Bactria & Indo-Greeks (post-Alexandrine, schematic)
properties.description: Graeco-Bactrian and Indo-Greek polities, coinage over Oxus-Indus; not continuous rule everywhere in box.
properties.sources: Bopearachchi, Indo-Greek, Indo-Scythian, and Indo-Parthian coins; Holt, Alexander; map coarse.
properties.imageCaption: Gold coin of Diodotos I of Bactria.jpg (Wikimedia).
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Teotihuacan state — highland central Mexico (core urban influence, schematic)
properties.description: Avenue of the Dead, multi-ethnic barrios, Tlaloc vs Quetzalcoatl; influence far (Maya) not fully shaded.
properties.sources: Carballo, Urbanization and religion in ancient Central Mexico; Cowgill, Teotihuacan; not obsidian route map.
properties.symbolism: Storm-god and feathered-serpent facades, warrior ceramics, and repeated urban axes: a multi-ethnic civic theatre of state religion.

properties.name: Zapotec state — Oaxaca & Monte Albán (schematic valley)
properties.description: Hilltop capital, calendric inscriptions, Classic–Epiclassic; expansion phases simplified.
properties.sources: Marcus, Zapotec civilization; Urcid, Script and society; valley polygon approximate.
properties.symbolism: Hilltop danzantes reliefs, calendric inscriptions, ballcourt sacrifice imagery: highland state theatre of military and cosmic order.

properties.name: Chacoan — Pueblo great houses & road network (N. New Mexico, schematic)
properties.description: Casa Rinconada, Great North Road, turquoise economy; not every outlier shown.
properties.sources: Lekson, The Chaco meridian; Crown & Judge, Chaco; regional box for pedagogy only.
properties.symbolism: Alignments, Great North Road, macaw and turquoise as exotic display, kiva floors: Chacoan religion tied cosmos to landscape and exchange.

properties.name: Funnel Beaker (TRB) — Northern & Central European megalith contact (schematic)
properties.description: First northern farmers, long barrows, causewayed enclosures; Corded/Bell Beaker later overlap; huge simplification.
properties.sources: Midgley, The Megaliths of Northern Europe; Prescott, Past imprints, coarse extent.
properties.symbolism: Barrows, enclosures, and solstice-leaning alignments: megalithic labour as communal memory and seasonal ritual.

properties.name: Pitted Ware & Battle Axe fringes (Baltic, schematic late Neolithic–EBA transition)
properties.description: Hunter-fisher-herder societies along Baltic with copper imports; not identical to Funnel Beaker or Corded Ware.
properties.sources: Apel, The coastal communities of the Late Middle Neolithic; Kristiansen, Europe Before History; box coarse.
properties.imageCaption: Gropkeramik (pitted ware) finds, Sweden (Nordisk familjebok, Commons).
properties.symbolism: Coastal pit-settlements, seal hunting, and ceramic temper pits: a northern Neolithic with distinct vessel grammar vs Corded and Funnel beakers.

properties.name: Nuragic Sardinia — nuraghe towers & villages (island block)
properties.description: Bronze age towers, well temples, Phoenician/Carthaginian late interaction; Punic not marked separately here.
properties.sources: Lilliu, La civiltà dei sardi; Lillie & Ellis, The Nuragic; geometry is entire island for teaching.
properties.symbolism: Cyclopean towers, well temples, bronze figurines: island-wide clan prestige; later Punic-influenced votives.

properties.name: Remote Oceania — late Polynesian & marginal expansion (schematic triangle, ca. 1000 BCE–1500 CE)
properties.description: East Polynesian voyaging, Aotearoa, marginal islands; not every archipelago; triangle for overview.
properties.sources: Kirch, On the road of the winds; Dening, Islets of the South Pacific; areal is intentionally broad.
properties.symbolism: Material, map, or monument imagery aligned with the teaching extent; see description and primary sources for detail.

properties.name: Uralic — Ural/forest homeland hypotheses (illustrative teaching box only)
properties.description: Proto-Uralic dispersal (multiple competing models: forest zone vs Volosovo–Garino lineages in debate). Not consensus geography.
properties.sources: Häkkinen, Early contacts; Salminen, Uralic languages; Sammallahti, Pajusalmi; for classroom contrast only.
properties.symbolism: From Finnic runic loans to Cyrillic and Latin today: forest and tundra lects share areal pressure from Germanic, Slavic, and Russian writing cultures.

properties.name: Japonic & related deep-time layers (Kyūshū–Honshū, schematic—modern bias avoided)
properties.description: Mainland & Ryūkyūan diversity; Ainu in Hokkaido distinct—shown as a north–south band for areal class use.
properties.sources: Vovin, Origins of the Japanese language; Pellard, Ryukyuan; not census-based.
properties.imageCaption: Genji monogatari emaki—kana beside Sinitic graphs (Commons).
properties.symbolism: Man’yōgana to kana syllabaries; court emaki pair word and image. Ainu katakana transcriptions later mark language documentation as display.

properties.name: Bantu—early Iron Age expansion corridors (sub-equatorial, deeply schematic)
properties.description: Linguists map multiple streams (east, south, west); this box is a single teaching super-layer—compare specialist maps.
properties.sources: Grollemund et al. (2015) PNAS expansion timing; Bostoen & Gregorio de Sousa, Prehistory; highly generalized.
properties.symbolism: Early Iron Age spread left vast oral networks; later scripts (colonial and national) overlay many communities—map is areal, not genetic script.

properties.name: Maya writing — classic lowland inscriptions (schematic Petén / north)
properties.description: Hieroglyphic text on stone, stucco, and bark; areal of literacy elite—not every Maya community or century.
properties.sources: Houston, Chinchilla Mazariegos, et al. (corpus and decipherment); Wichmann, languages of the region (generalized box).
properties.imageCaption: Maya glyphs in a classic epigraphy plate (Commons).
properties.symbolism: Logosyllabic texts on stelae, lintels, and bark paper: calendrics, royal names, and verb morphology make stone and book a single political medium.

properties.name: Ogham — insular epigraphic habit (Ireland, schematic)
properties.description: Notched stones and occasional wood; late Roman/early medieval Ireland—illustrative box, not every find-spot.
properties.sources: Macalister, Corpus Inscriptionum Insularum Celticarum (legacy); McManus, Guide to Ogam; Ireland-focused.
properties.symbolism: Edge-notched Irish letters on stone markers; a local script idiom alongside Latin on later crosses—monumental naming in a compressed alphabet.

properties.name: Gəʿəz — Ethiopic manuscript culture (Horn, schematic)
properties.description: Abugida for liturgy, royal chronicles, and highland Christianity—extent is a teaching band, not modern borders.
properties.sources: Hudson, Languages of Ethiopia; Getatchew, scribal history (generalized).
properties.symbolism: Gəʿəz as church and state graphy; abugida structure links Semitic consonants to vocalic color—visible liturgy across the Horn.

properties.name: Glagolitic & early Cyrillic — mission Slavia (schematic)
properties.description: Liturgical first-writing for Slavic, then Cyrillic diffusion—one box for teaching contrast with Latin/Greek spheres.
properties.sources: Cubberley, The alphabets of Europe; Schenker, Slavic; simplified Balkan–Slavic band.
properties.symbolism: Glagolitic invention, then Cyrillic: Slavic liturgy made local sound visible; rival Greek and Latin scripts frame a long battle of alphabets.

properties.name: Parthian Arsacid polity (Iran + Mesopotamia core, schematic)
properties.description: Equestrian lords, Greek façades, and a silk-road node network—not a uniform province map.
properties.sources: Colledge, The Parthians; Sinisi & Köhn, Empires of the Silk Road; simplified.
properties.symbolism: Equestrian lords, rock reliefs, and a silk-road pluralism: Iranian kingship in dialogue with Hellenic façades and Asian trade cities.

properties.name: Kushan Empire (trans-Hindu Kush & north India, schematic)
properties.description: Gandhara, Mathura, and Central Asian emporia—borders vary by source.
properties.sources: Liu, The Silk Road in world history; Rosenfield, The dynastic art of the Kushans; generalised box.
properties.symbolism: Buddha coinage, Greco-Roman atelier styles, and Gandharan stūpa: steppe cavalry + Indian urbanisation as visible dharma display.

properties.name: Scythian & related Ponto–Caspian steppe (schematic)
properties.description: Kurgan burials, animal-style art, and long-range exchange—not a single fixed border.
properties.sources: Cunliffe, The Scythians; Parzinger, Die Skythen; areal is illustrative.
properties.symbolism: Kurgan burials, animal-style metalwork, and long-range relay exchange: Ponto-Caspian prestige without a single imperial capital.

properties.name: Hopewell — interaction sphere, Ohio & tributaries (schematic)
properties.description: Earthwork landscapes, exotics, and mortuary mounds; not a political empire.
properties.sources: Milner, The Moundbuilders; Charles & Buikstra, Hopewell; basin box only.
properties.symbolism: Riverside earthworks, mica and copper from afar, and long-distance ‘interaction’ without Mesoamerican state forms.

properties.name: Nok culture — central Nigerian plateau (schematic)
properties.description: Terracotta and early iron; extent debated—illustrative band.
properties.sources: Fagg, Nok terracottas; Breunig et al. iron; simplified.
properties.symbolism: Lifesize heads, iron smelting debate, and early West African figuration—symbolic power before the Sahel’s later empires.

properties.name: Ban Chiang & related neolithic–bronze (NE Thailand, schematic)
properties.description: Painted pottery, metallurgy sequences; not the whole of Southeast Asia.
properties.sources: UNESCO, Ban Chiang; Higham, Early mainland Southeast Asia; localised box.
properties.symbolism: Painted pottery, early metallurgy, and mortuary rites: a mainland Southeast Asian narrative distinct from the Yellow River only.

properties.name: Maritime Srivijaya-linked sphere (Sumatra & Malacca strait, schematic)
properties.description: Buddha-ship inscriptions, entrepots, and hinterland polities; boundaries unclear.
properties.sources: Coedès, The Indianized states; Wolters, Srivijaya; teaching band.
properties.symbolism: Buddha mandala architecture, strait emporia, and Sanskrit titles on Sumatra and Java: thalassocracy with temple receipts.

properties.name: Lanna & northern Thai highland mandala (schematic, post-classical)
properties.description: Sukhothai and Lan Na chronicles overlap; box is a north Thai teaching layer.
properties.sources: Wyatt, Thailand: A short history; Grabowsky, Introduction to northern Thai; generalised.
properties.imageCaption: Wat Phra That Doi Suthep, Chiang Mai (Commons).
properties.symbolism: Chedi gold umbrellas, dhamma-sāsana inscriptions, and highland vassalage: a Tai-Buddhist visual order alongside Burmese and Khmer worlds.

properties.name: Swahili coast — stone town culture (E. Africa, schematic band)
properties.description: Coral-rag cities, dhow monsoons, and Swahili as a public written language; not a single realm.
properties.sources: Horton & Middleton, The Swahili; Allen, The Swahili house; areal is broad.
properties.symbolism: Coral rag mosques, merchant chronicles, and monsoon scripts: the coast as a bilingual Persian–Arabic–Bantu public sphere.

properties.name: Late La Tène & Celtic oppidum belt (Gaul, schematic)
properties.description: Bibracte-type towns and Romanward diplomacy before Caesar’s narrative peak.
properties.sources: Cunliffe, The Ancient Celts; Fichtl, Les Gaulois; western European band.
properties.symbolism: Hill-fort renaissance, late La Tène art, and Roman-facing diplomacy: ‘Celtic’ as urbanising Iron Age, not a romantic stereotype only.

properties.name: Seleucid kingdoms — from Syria to Bactria (schematic, not stable borders)
properties.description: Macedonian diadochi state(s) in flux; Bactrian breakaway and Parthian rise shrink the old core—illustrative band.
properties.sources: Sherwin-White & Kuhrt, From Samarkhand to Sardis; Capdetrey, L’économie séleucide; generalised map.
properties.symbolism: Basilikoi, refounded poleis, and Iranian satrapies under Greek royal titulature: a patchwork of cities and camps, not a Roman census map.

properties.name: Sasanian Iranshahr (Iran–Mesopotamia core, schematic)
properties.description: Ctesiphon-centred polity with Mesopotamian and Khorāsān depths; not every frontier year.
properties.sources: Daryaee, Sasanian Persia; Canepa, The Two Eyes of the Earth; areal is teaching-grade.
properties.symbolism: Ātashkadeh courts, Shapurian rock investitures, and Middle Persian epigraphy: Iranshahr as cosmo-religious theatre on Rome’s eastern rim.

properties.name: Neo-Assyrian — Upper Mesopotamia heartland (schematic)
properties.description: Nimrud–Nineveh–Khorsabad axis; vassal stripes beyond the Assyrian triangle omitted here.
properties.sources: Grayson, Assyria; Radner, The neo-Assyrian empire; not a full provincial map.
properties.symbolism: Lamassu, deportation inscriptions, and state workshops: terror-and-gift as legible power along the Tigris corridor.

properties.name: Moche — north coast Andean (schematic river valleys)
properties.description: Huaca-based polities, irrigation, and regalia; not one unified Moche ‘empire’ map.
properties.sources: Donnan & McClelland, Moche fineline painting; Dillehay, The Oxford handbook of the Andes; box is a coastal band.
properties.imageCaption: Moche portrait vessel (Commons / Art Institute of Chicago).
properties.symbolism: Stirrup-spout pots, regalia in clay, and Andean water cult debate: a coastal theocracy in ceramic, not a single imperial grid.

properties.name: Nazca & south-coast pampa (schematic, geoglyph locale)
properties.description: Geoglyph fields and Cahuachi–Nasca ritual geography—coarse pampa box.
properties.sources: Proulx, A sourcebook; Silverman, Cahuachi; Lozada & Ogburn, et al.; areal is simplified.
properties.symbolism: Pampa geoglyphs, qanat-linked ritual fields, and Paracas forebears: arid-surface processions and water politics as public geometry.

properties.name: Adena — mound-complex, Ohio & upper tributaries (schematic)
properties.description: Conical mounds, copper, long-distance exotics; overlaps later Hopewell in places—simplified heartland only.
properties.sources: Dragoo, Mounds for the dead; Seeman, The Ohio Hopewell/Adena literature; teaching polygon.
properties.symbolism: Conical mounds, copper celts, and log-tomb inhumations: Ohio Valley mortuary bling before the full Hopewell exchange surge.

properties.name: Hunnic & allied confederations — Ponto-Asiatic steppe (schematic, Attila era)
properties.description: Military coalitions, Roman diplomacy, and shifting clan blocks—not a single continuous realm.
properties.sources: Maenchen-Helfen, The world of the Huns; Pohl, in Cambridge History of the Goths; map is a teaching band.
properties.imageCaption: Roman and Hunnic zones, c. 450, schematic (Commons).
properties.symbolism: Saddle diplomacy, gold diadems, and Roman subventions: steppe confederation politics, not a stable taxation boundary.

properties.name: Phoenician & Canaanite coast — long-distance network (schematic)
properties.description: Byblos, Tyre, Sidon, and outposts: maritime levant, not a unitary state.
properties.sources: Aubet, The Phoenicians; Markoe, The Phoenicians; coastal strip and islands omitted beyond teaching scope.
properties.symbolism: Tyrian purple, oared commerce, and pilot alphabets: a city-state lattice of harbours, not a unified inland empire.

properties.name: Nanzhao & Dali precursors — Yunnan highland mandala (schematic)
properties.description: Bai, Yi, and Nanzhao / Dali chronology overlap; not a Han map.
properties.sources: Backus, The Nan-chao; Herman, The kingdom of Dali; box around Erhai basin, broadly.
properties.symbolism: Buddha-bridge stūpa rows, Dali inscriptions, and Yunnan–Tibetan–Chinese overlap: a highland Tai-Bai corridor, not a Han prefecture read.

properties.name: East Polynesian expansion — Rapa Nui, Mangareva, Society zone (broad teaching band)
properties.description: Ahu megaliths, chiefdom networks, and archipelago colonisation timing—huge water distances; box is a schematic arc.
properties.sources: Kirch, On the road of the winds; Wilmshurst et al. radiocarbon debate; areal is intentionally large.
properties.symbolism: Ahu platforms, star-compass seafaring, and tapu lineage: East Polynesian politics as re-established sacred geography, not a European island chart.
